posts.p_message: War Rapidly Escalates — MeidasTouch Network and Ben Meiselas Mar 1 This war is growing in real time and the consequences are already reaching far beyond the initial strikes. The Trump regime started a war, but it appears they didn’t think at all as to what to do after bombing Iran and killing the country’s leadership. We now have confirmed American casualties. Three U.S. service members have been killed in action. Five more are seriously wounded. Several others suffered concussions and shrapnel injuries. These are the first U.S. fatalities of the war, and they are the direct result of Iranian retaliation. Trump’s Ambassador to the U.N. Mike Waltz responded to the news of American deaths with a callous tweet. “Freedom is never free,” he wrote, along with an American flag emoji. We do not yet know the exact locations where the Americans were killed. What we do know is that U.S. military installations across the region — including in Bahrain, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates — have been hit by ballistic missiles and attack drones. Iran’s response has not been symbolic. It has been lethal and regional. Across Israel, at least nine people have been killed in missile strikes. In central Israel, including the city of Beit Shemesh, additional deaths and missing persons are being reported after Iranian strikes. Across the Gulf, including the UAE, Qatar, Kuwait, Bahrain, and Oman, more civilians have died as Iran’s retaliation expands to countries hosting U.S. forces. Drone strikes have hit strategic infrastructure. A French naval facility in Abu Dhabi was targeted. Explosions have been reported at Zayed Port, critical deep-water commercial port serving Abu Dhabi. Missile and drone activity has also shaken cities once considered insulated from direct conflict, including Dubai. There are also indications that oil infrastructure in the Persian Gulf has been struck. Iran has also launched attacks on U.S. positions in Iraq, and one American base in Kuwait was reportedly hit. Meanwhile, Israel is expanding operations. Israeli forces say they are conducting ongoing airstrikes directly over Tehran and now claim full air superiority over the Iranian capital. The United States has escalated as well. CENTCOM confirmed that an Iranian warship struck early in the conflict is now sinking in the Gulf of Oman. Inside Iran, the civilian toll continues to climb. At least 133 civilians have reportedly been killed and more than 200 wounded. Among them were dozens of children killed when a girls’ elementary school near a naval facility was struck. I feel I must keep emphasizing that this is not contained. Much of the Middle East is now being pulled in. Iran is now being run by an interim leadership committee following the killing of the Ayatollah. Iranian leadership says that his death will not deter them. Their national security leadership has vowed retaliation against U.S. and Israeli targets “with a force they have never experienced before.” They are already carrying out additional waves of attacks. Iranian officials say new phases of operations targeting U.S. bases are underway. And the conflict is spreading in ways that extend beyond traditional military confrontation. Mass pro-regime protests have erupted across Iran, with crowds calling for revenge. Demonstrations have also taken place outside U.S. diplomatic facilities abroad. In Karachi, protesters stormed the U.S. consulate compound, leading to deadly clashes with security forces. Clashes have broken out in Iraq. Saudi Arabia has authorized its military to respond to Iranian attacks if necessary. Meanwhile, U.S. intelligence agencies are warning about potential asymmetric retaliation, including the activation of proxy networks or sleeper cells. That concern has already prompted heightened vigilance at home. Early this morning, a horrifying mass shooting in Austin, Texas left three people dead and fourteen injured. Federal authorities are investigating whether the attack may have links to terrorism. The FBI has activated its counterterrorism task force and confirmed there are indicators being examined for a possible nexus. The risk environment is no longer confined to the Middle East. Back in the region, infrastructure critical to the global economy is now under strain. The Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly 20 percent of the world’s oil and liquefied natural gas supply flows, has effectively ground to a halt. Iranian forces have reportedly fired near commercial vessels. At least 150 tankers are now anchored in open Gulf waters, with dozens more stalled on the opposite side of the chokepoint. Shipping is being rerouted. Airspace across parts of the Gulf has closed. Oil markets are expected to react dramatically. At the same time, humanitarian crises are deepening. Israel has closed crossings into Gaza, including for humanitarian aid workers. Civilian casualties continue there even as political leaders speak publicly about ceasefires that have not materialized on the ground. Meanwhile, inside Iran, public anger has intensified rather than dissipated. Rather than collapsing after the Ayatollah’s death, the regime’s influence, including through allied groups across the region, remains intact. Intelligence agencies had warned that removing a single leader would not dismantle the system. Instead, Iran’s leadership is consolidating. Trump had reportedly anticipated a short conflict — a matter of days — believing that killing the Ayatollah would quickly force Tehran to the negotiating table. There was even a quiet ceasefire proposal sent through intermediaries after the strike. Iran rejected it. You read that right. Trump apparently thought after killing Iran’s Supreme Leader, that they would eagerly take up his offer for a ceasefire. Trump is now publicly suggesting Iran’s new leadership wants talks, but Iran’s foreign minister has indicated diplomacy may no longer be realistic after U.S. strikes derailed ongoing nuclear negotiations. There are also reports that former Iranian president Mahmoud Ahmadinejad has been killed in a strike. The conflict is already expanding militarily, politically, and economically. Officials are warning this operation could last weeks at minimum. Lindsey Graham also made clear that the administration is operating without a defined post-war framework, arguing that it is not the president’s responsibility to shape what comes next inside Iran. “Our goal is to make sure Iran cannot become again the largest state sponsor of terrorism,” Graham told NBC’s Kristen Welker. “Does the the president have a plan to guarantee that happens?” Welker asked. Graham responded, “No. It's not his job.” Not his job?!! Meanwhile, the American people are overwhelmingly against Trump’s war. Only a quarter of Americans support Trump’s strike on Iran, per a new Reuters/Ipsos poll. And even as the conflict escalates abroad, the domestic propaganda machinery continues, with banners featuring far-right activist Charlie Kirk now hanging from the Department of Education building. As of now, there is no clear endgame in sight in Iran. We are again embroiled in another messy war in the Middle East. We will continue bringing you updates as this unfolds.

posts.p_message: Yesterday Morning, the first American and Israeli strikes targeted Iran. In a video he posted online, Donald Trump announced that the United States had launched major combat operations against Iran, named Operation Epic Fury (coordinated with Israel's Roaring Lion strikes). He said the goal is to destroy Iran's missile and naval capabilities, eliminate the nuclear threat, and protect the U.S. and its allies. He directly addressed the Iranian people, urging them to overthrow their government: "The hour of your freedom is at hand. When we are finished, take over your government. It will be yours to take."He warned that the operation will be massive, will last several days, and that American lives may be lost. ~09:30–10:00 IRST First explosions reported in Tehran and other Iranian cities (Isfahan, Qom, Tabriz mentioned early). Israeli preemptive strikes (Operation Roaring Lion) begin, quickly supported by US forces (Operation Epic Fury). Witnesses hear blasts near leadership sites (Khamenei's compound area, central Tehran). Videos show thick black smoke rising over University Street/Jomhouri districts; air defenses activate. Panic in streets, people filming from rooftops/balconies. ~10:00–11:00 IRST Strikes intensify across Iran, multiple waves hit Tehran (presidential areas, IRGC sites), plus southern Minab (school strike alleged later), Kermanshah, Karaj. Iranian state media broadcasts smoke plumes and damage. Footage circulates: large explosions in residential/military zones, craters visible, people fleeing or taking cover. ~11:30–12:30 IRST : Iran launches first retaliatory wave, ballistic missiles and drones toward Israel. Sirens across Jerusalem, Tel Aviv, Haifa. Iron Dome intercepts many; videos show bright mid-air explosions, booms over northern Israel (Haifa bay area), people rushing to shelters. ~12:30–13:30 IRST : Trump posts 8-minute video on Truth Social announcing "major combat operations" are underway. He details destroying missiles/navy, warns of US losses, urges Iranians: "The hour of your freedom is at hand... take over your government." ~13:30–15:30 IRST : Iranian counterstrikes expand to US/allied bases. Missiles/drones target Al Udeid (Qatar), Bahrain (5th Fleet HQ), Al Dhafra (UAE), Ali Al Salem (Kuwait), Jordan sites, Saudi areas. Explosions/smoke in Manama, Doha vicinity, Abu Dhabi. Videos: plumes near Bahrain naval base, interceptions over Gulf skies, sounds of impacts in Kuwait. Many intercepted; limited damage reported. ~15:30 IRST onward : Multiple iranian strikes against US bases in the middle East, as well as continuous US and Israeli strikes inside Iran. Multiple videos slowly emerging into the informational chaos. WHAT WAS TARGETED IN IRAN 🇮🇷 : 🟧Tehran (primary target – multiple waves) 🔸Compound / offices of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei (city center, smoke visible on satellite, main building destroyed) 🔸Presidential Palace / offices (President Pezeshkian) 🔸University Street (missile strikes confirmed) 🔸Jomhouri District 🔸Seyyed Khandan District (north) 🔸Southern Tehran Ministries (multiple buildings) 🔸Revolutionary Guard Headquarters / sites 🔸Mehrabad Airport area 🔸Political and security leadership sites (IDF confirmed) 🟧Isfahan- 🔸IsfahanNuclear Technology Center 🔸Nuclear Enrichment Complex (one of the 3 nuclear sites targeted by US bombers) 🔸Military and defense sites 🟧Qom: 🔸Command and military sites (explosions confirmed) 🟧Karaj Missile production facilities 🔸Defense and military sites 🟧Kermanshah System Advanced SA-65 air defense system 🔸Western military sites 🟧Tabriz: 🔸Military sites and IRGC 🟧Shiraz: 🔸Military sites (reported by Iranian media) 🟧Ilam: 🔸Defense infrastructure 🟧Lorestan/Khorramabad: 🔸Military sites 🟧Bushehr: 🔸Port + possible nuclear or oil-related facilities 🟧Minab (Hormozgan province, south): 🔸Girls' primary school (strike confirmed – 40 to 53 civilian deaths according to IRNA) 🟧Asaluyeh (Persian Gulf port): 🔸Critical oil and port facilities 🟧Other confirmed nuclear sites: 🔸Fordow Fuel Enrichment Plant (Fordo) 🔸Natanz Enrichment Complex 🟧Other mentions: Hamedan (explosions reported) 🟧Jask (civilian casualties mentioned). Several Gulf port cities and ballistic missile sites (not all publicly named or geolocated). WHAT IRAN TARGETED IN RETALIATION : 🟦Israel 🇮🇱 (primary target – multiple waves) 🔹Northern areas (Haifa Bay/Haifa district – explosions confirmed, residential building hit in Tirat Carmel) 🔹Tel Aviv (missiles near the city, sirens everywhere) 🔹Jerusalem and central Israel (massive barrages, massive Iron Dome interceptions) 🔹Other cities with sirens: throughout the country (mainly north and central Israel) 🟦Bahrain🇧🇭 (Manama) 🔹Naval Support Activity Bahrain/5th Fleet HQ (Juffair district – explosions and smoke seen confirmed, strike verified by multiple media outlets) 🔹Coastal areas around the naval base 🟦Qatar 🇶🇦 (near Doha) 🔹Al Udeid Air Base (largest US base in the Middle East – priority target, massive interceptions but missiles launched at it) 🟦United Arab Emirates 🇦🇪 (UAE) 🔹Al Dhafra Air Base (Abu Dhabi – major US air base) 🔹Residential areas in Abu Dhabi/Dubai (missile debris) – 1 civilian killed (confirmed by Emirati media, airports closed) 🟦Kuwait:🇰🇼 🔹Ali Al Salem Air Base (“The Rock” – key US air base, interceptions but directly targeted) 🟦Jordan 🇯🇴 : 🔹US air bases (Muwaffaq Salti Air Base and other sites – missiles intercepted, strikes confirmed by Amman) 🟦Iraq: 🇮🇶 🔹US bases in the north (including Ain al-Asad and associated sites – mentioned in IRGC statements) 🔹Jurf al-Sakhar (other US installations) 🟦Saudi Arabia 🇸🇦: 🔹US bases in Riad 🟦Syria 🇸🇾: 🔹Minor missiles/drones debris/impacts (Suwayda – 4 civilians killed according to local reports) 🟦USA 🇺🇸 : 🔹Multiple bases in the middle East 🔹Potential strikes against naval assets in the region (to confirm) WHAT ARE THE RESULTS of US/Israeli strikes? ⬜️Damage to key Iranian targets: ▫️Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei's compound in Tehran heavily damaged (satellite imagery shows destroyed buildings and smoke plumes). ▫️Presidential offices and other leadership/political sites in Tehran hit. ▫️Nuclear-related facilities targeted (e.g., Isfahan nuclear technology center, Fordow, Natanz enrichment sites). ▫️Military/IRGC bases, ballistic missile sites, and defense infrastructure struck across multiple cities (Tehran, Isfahan, Qom, Karaj, Kermanshah, Tabriz, Bushehr, etc.). ⬜️Casualties in Iran (confirmed/reported): ▫️Dozens killed and wounded in southern strikes (Hormozgan province): 70+ killed and 90+ wounded in Minab and Jask combined (Iranian Red Crescent/Fars News). ▫️Major civilian incident: Strike on girls' elementary school in Minab killed 40–63 students (to confirm) ▫️At least 2 students killed in a strike in Tehran (reported). ▫️Several senior IRGC/military officials killed (Israeli sources claim 3+; exact names/confirmation limited). ⬜️Status of key figures: ▫️President Masoud Pezeshkian alive (as per Iranian officials), however, this could be false. ▫️Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei: Status unclear/conflicting — Iranian FM says "alive as far as I know"; Israeli sources/TV report "likely killed" or cut off from contact; no public appearance yet. ⬜️Overall impact on Iran: ▫️Widespread explosions/smoke in Tehran and other cities; airspace closed; significant degradation claimed to missile/naval capabilities (US/Israel statements). WHAT ARE THE WAR GOAL ? ♦️Regime change in Teheran ♦️Final destruction of nuclear and missile program ♦️Destruction of Iranian Navy WHAT ARE THE DIPLOMATIC REACTIONS ? 🔹Saudi Arabia 🇸🇦 : Strongly condemns Iran's missile attacks on Gulf states (including Riyadh/Eastern Province, intercepted); full solidarity with UAE/Bahrain/Qatar/Kuwait/Jordan; warns of grave consequences. 🔹UAE 🇦🇪 : Calls conflict an "historic moment"; criticizes global leaders for failing regional stability; 1 civilian killed by debris in Abu Dhabi. 🔹Qatar 🇶🇦 : Activated defenses at Al Udeid base; focused on repelling attacks and sovereignty protection. 🔹Bahrain 🇧🇭 : Condemns Iranian strikes near 5th Fleet HQ; explosions intercepted. 🔹Kuwait 🇰🇼 : Intercepted missiles at Ali Al Salem base; condemns sovereignty violations. 🔹Jordan 🇯🇴 : Engaged defenses against Iranian missiles; condemns blatant violations. 🔹Israel 🇮🇱 : Defends strikes as preemptive to eliminate existential threat from regime/missiles/proxies. 🔹United States 🇺🇸 : Trump announces "major combat operations" to destroy missiles/navy, prevent nuclear threat, urges regime change. 🔹EU 🇪🇺🇫🇷🇩🇪🇬🇧... (incl. France/Germany/UK) : Urges maximum restraint, civilian protection, respect for international law; calls for resumed negotiations; Macron seeks urgent UNSC meeting. 🔹United Nations 🇺🇳 : Guterres condemns escalation; calls for immediate cessation; Human Rights chief deplores strikes/retaliation. 🔹Russia 🇷🇺 : Condemns US/Israel strikes as unprovoked aggression; warns of humanitarian/economic/radiological catastrophe; Medvedev mocks "peacemaker" Trump. 🔹China 🇨🇳 : Highly concerned; calls for immediate halt to military actions and return to negotiations/diplomacy. 🔹Turkiye 🇹🇷 : Urges all parties to cease hostilities immediately; risks to regional/global stability; suspended flights to several Mideast countries. 🔹Egypt 🇪🇬 : Strongly condemns Iran's attacks on Arab states' unity/integrity; warns of grave risks to Arab security/stability. 🔹Iran 🇮🇷 : Accuses US/Israel of UN Charter violation; vows crushing/harsh response; demands urgent UNSC action. Early this morning, the first american and israeli strikes targeted Iran. In a video he posted online, Donald Trump announced that the United States had launched major combat operations against Iran, named Operation Epic Fury (coordinated with Israel's Roaring Lion strikes). He said the goal is to destroy Iran's missile and naval capabilities, eliminate the nuclear threat, and protect the U.S. and its allies. He directly addressed the Iranian people, urging them to overthrow their government: "The hour of your freedom is at hand. When we are finished, take over your government. It will be yours to take."He warned that the operation will be massive, will last several days, and that American lives may be lost. ~09:30–10:00 IRST First explosions reported in Tehran and other Iranian cities (Isfahan, Qom, Tabriz mentioned early). Israeli preemptive strikes (Operation Roaring Lion) begin, quickly supported by US forces (Operation Epic Fury). Witnesses hear blasts near leadership sites (Khamenei's compound area, central Tehran). Videos show thick black smoke rising over University Street/Jomhouri districts; air defenses activate. Panic in streets, people filming from rooftops/balconies. ~10:00–11:00 IRST Strikes intensify across Iran, multiple waves hit Tehran (presidential areas, IRGC sites), plus southern Minab (school strike alleged later), Kermanshah, Karaj. Iranian state media broadcasts smoke plumes and damage. Footage circulates: large explosions in residential/military zones, craters visible, people fleeing or taking cover. ~11:30–12:30 IRST : Iran launches first retaliatory wave, ballistic missiles and drones toward Israel. Sirens across Jerusalem, Tel Aviv, Haifa. Iron Dome intercepts many; videos show bright mid-air explosions, booms over northern Israel (Haifa bay area), people rushing to shelters. ~12:30–13:30 IRST : Trump posts 8-minute video on Truth Social announcing "major combat operations" are underway. Many intercepted; limited damage reported. ~15:30 IRST onward : Multiple iranian strikes against US bases in the middle East, as well as continuous US and Israeli strikes inside Iran. He details destroying missiles/navy, warns of US losses, urges Iranians: "The hour of your freedom is at hand... take over your government." ~13:30–15:30 IRST : Iranian counterstrikes expand to US/allied bases. Missiles/drones target Al Udeid (Qatar), Bahrain (5th Fleet HQ), Al Dhafra (UAE), Ali Al Salem (Kuwait), Jordan sites, Saudi areas. Explosions/smoke in Manama, Doha vicinity, Abu Dhabi. Videos: plumes near Bahrain naval base, interceptions over Gulf skies, sounds of impacts in Kuwait. Many intercepted; limited damage reported. ~15:30 IRST onward : Multiple iranian strikes against US bases in the middle East, as well as continuous US and Israeli strikes inside Iran. Multiple videos slowly emerging into the informational chaos. WHAT WAS TARGETED IN IRAN 🇮🇷 : 🟧Tehran (primary target – multiple waves) 🔸Compound / offices of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei (city center, smoke visible on satellite, main building destroyed) 🔸Presidential Palace / offices (President Pezeshkian) 🔸University Street (missile strikes confirmed) 🔸Jomhouri District 🔸Seyyed Khandan District (north) 🔸Southern Tehran Ministries (multiple buildings) 🔸Revolutionary Guard Headquarters / sites 🔸Mehrabad Airport area 🔸Political and security leadership sites (IDF confirmed) 🟧Isfahan- 🔸IsfahanNuclear Technology Center 🔸Nuclear Enrichment Complex (one of the 3 nuclear sites targeted by US bombers) 🔸Military and defense sites 🟧Qom: 🔸Command and military sites (explosions confirmed) 🟧Karaj Missile production facilities 🔸Defense and military sites 🟧Kermanshah System Advanced SA-65 air defense system 🔸Western military sites 🟧Tabriz: 🔸Military sites and IRGC 🟧Shiraz: 🔸Military sites (reported by Iranian media) 🟧Ilam: 🔸Defense infrastructure 🟧Lorestan/Khorramabad: 🔸Military sites 🟧Bushehr: 🔸Port + possible nuclear or oil-related facilities 🟧Minab (Hormozgan province, south): 🔸Girls' primary school (strike confirmed – 40 to 53 civilian deaths according to IRNA) 🟧Asaluyeh (Persian Gulf port): 🔸Critical oil and port facilities 🟧Other confirmed nuclear sites: 🔸Fordow Fuel Enrichment Plant (Fordo) 🔸Natanz Enrichment Complex 🟧Other mentions: Hamedan (explosions reported) 🟧Jask (civilian casualties mentioned). Several Gulf port cities and ballistic missile sites (not all publicly named or geolocated). WHAT IRAN TARGETED IN RETALIATION : 🟦Israel 🇮🇱 (primary target – multiple waves) 🔹Northern areas (Haifa Bay/Haifa district – explosions confirmed, residential building hit in Tirat Carmel) 🔹Tel Aviv (missiles near the city, sirens everywhere) 🔹Jerusalem and central Israel (massive barrages, massive Iron Dome interceptions) 🔹Other cities with sirens: throughout the country (mainly north and central Israel) 🟦Bahrain🇧🇭 (Manama) 🔹Naval Support Activity Bahrain/5th Fleet HQ (Juffair district – explosions and smoke seen confirmed, strike verified by multiple media outlets) 🔹Coastal areas around the naval base 🟦Qatar 🇶🇦 (near Doha) 🔹Al Udeid Air Base (largest US base in the Middle East – priority target, massive interceptions but missiles launched at it) 🟦United Arab Emirates 🇦🇪 (UAE) 🔹Al Dhafra Air Base (Abu Dhabi – major US air base) 🔹Residential areas in Abu Dhabi/Dubai (missile debris) – 1 civilian killed (confirmed by Emirati media, airports closed) 🟦Kuwait:🇰🇼 🔹Ali Al Salem Air Base (“The Rock” – key US air base, interceptions but directly targeted) 🟦Jordan 🇯🇴 : 🔹US air bases (Muwaffaq Salti Air Base and other sites – missiles intercepted, strikes confirmed by Amman) 🟦Iraq: 🇮🇶 🔹US bases in the north (including Ain al-Asad and associated sites – mentioned in IRGC statements) 🔹Jurf al-Sakhar (other US installations) 🟦Saudi Arabia 🇸🇦: 🔹US bases in Riad 🟦Syria 🇸🇾: 🔹Minor missiles/drones debris/impacts (Suwayda – 4 civilians killed according to local reports) 🟦USA 🇺🇸 : 🔹Multiple bases in the middle East 🔹Potential strikes against naval assets in the region (to confirm) WHAT ARE THE RESULTS of US/Israeli strikes? ⬜️Damage to key Iranian targets: ▫️Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei's compound in Tehran heavily damaged (satellite imagery shows destroyed buildings and smoke plumes). ▫️Presidential offices and other leadership/political sites in Tehran hit. ▫️Nuclear-related facilities targeted (e.g., Isfahan nuclear technology center, Fordow, Natanz enrichment sites). ▫️Military/IRGC bases, ballistic missile sites, and defense infrastructure struck across multiple cities (Tehran, Isfahan, Qom, Karaj, Kermanshah, Tabriz, Bushehr, etc.). ⬜️Casualties in Iran (confirmed/reported): ▫️Dozens killed and wounded in southern strikes (Hormozgan province): 70+ killed and 90+ wounded in Minab and Jask combined (Iranian Red Crescent/Fars News). ▫️Major civilian incident: Strike on girls' elementary school in Minab killed 40–63 students (to confirm) ▫️At least 2 students killed in a strike in Tehran (reported). ▫️Several senior IRGC/military officials killed (Israeli sources claim 3+; exact names/confirmation limited). ⬜️Status of key figures: ▫️President Masoud Pezeshkian alive (as per Iranian officials), however, this could be false. ▫️Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei: Status unclear/conflicting — Iranian FM says "alive as far as I know"; Israeli sources/TV report "likely killed" or cut off from contact; no public appearance yet. ⬜️Overall impact on Iran: ▫️Widespread explosions/smoke in Tehran and other cities; airspace closed; significant degradation claimed to missile/naval capabilities (US/Israel statements). WHAT ARE THE WAR GOAL ? ♦️Regime change in Teheran ♦️Final destruction of nuclear and missile program ♦️Destruction of Iranian Navy WHAT ARE THE DIPLOMATIC REACTIONS ? 🔹Saudi Arabia 🇸🇦 : Strongly condemns Iran's missile attacks on Gulf states (including Riyadh/Eastern Province, intercepted); full solidarity with UAE/Bahrain/Qatar/Kuwait/Jordan; warns of grave consequences. 🔹UAE 🇦🇪 : Calls conflict an "historic moment"; criticizes global leaders for failing regional stability; 1 civilian killed by debris in Abu Dhabi. 🔹Qatar 🇶🇦 : Activated defenses at Al Udeid base; focused on repelling attacks and sovereignty protection. 🔹Bahrain 🇧🇭 : Condemns Iranian strikes near 5th Fleet HQ; explosions intercepted. 🔹Kuwait 🇰🇼 : Intercepted missiles at Ali Al Salem base; condemns sovereignty violations. 🔹Jordan 🇯🇴 : Engaged defenses against Iranian missiles; condemns blatant violations. 🔹Israel 🇮🇱 : Defends strikes as preemptive to eliminate existential threat from regime/missiles/proxies. 🔹United States 🇺🇸 : Trump announces "major combat operations" to destroy missiles/navy, prevent nuclear threat, urges regime change. 🔹EU 🇪🇺🇫🇷🇩🇪🇬🇧... (incl. France/Germany/UK) : Urges maximum restraint, civilian protection, respect for international law; calls for resumed negotiations; Macron seeks urgent UNSC meeting. 🔹United Nations 🇺🇳 : Guterres condemns escalation; calls for immediate cessation; Human Rights chief deplores strikes/retaliation. 🔹Russia 🇷🇺 : Condemns US/Israel strikes as unprovoked aggression; warns of humanitarian/economic/radiological catastrophe; Medvedev mocks "peacemaker" Trump. 🔹China 🇨🇳 : Highly concerned; calls for immediate halt to military actions and return to negotiations/diplomacy. 🔹Turkiye 🇹🇷 : Urges all parties to cease hostilities immediately; risks to regional/global stability; suspended flights to several Mideast countries. 🔹Egypt 🇪🇬 : Strongly condemns Iran's attacks on Arab states' unity/integrity; warns of grave risks to Arab security/stability. 🔹Iran 🇮🇷 : Accuses US/Israel of UN Charter violation; vows crushing/harsh response; demands urgent UNSC action.

posts.p_message: 250410 – Today we are back to Dubai – a place we visited in 2020 (we think – memories are fading). After our not-so-great experiences in India, this city is such a breath of fresh air (literally). Drivers actually obey traffic rules which makes for a very nice ride around town. What we missed the last time here was a trip to the Burj Khalifa building. It is currently the highest building in the world, but we hear that Abu Dhabi will soon beat it by having a building a couple of feet taller. Although the Burj Khalifa is reportedly at 163 floors, we can only go (by elevator) to Floor 124, which was plenty enough for us. I thought the Taipei Tower 101 was scary --- Burj Khalifa beats it. The building itself and the shopping mall are the cleanest, most modern and organized we have experience. I was really surprised at the number and types of stores and restaurants (somewhere north of 1,200) we usually find somewhere in the USA. I actually broke my own rule of eating in town (PAYING for eating instead of eating on the ship for FREE), but when I saw the Krispy Kream donut shop, I told Julieann “That's where we're going next.” That was before I saw the Philly Steak house. WTH? It's been “forever” since I've had a Philly Steak Sandwich and this one (with the loaded fries) hit the magical spot. Of course, I did save enough room for one Krispy Kream donut. The view of Dubai from Burj Khalifa's 124 floor was amazing. Our photos don't do it justice. BUT the wait in line most probably takes up to two or three hours for most visitors. I don't know exactly how long the wait was because after we had already stood in line for about an hour, some nice lady that works in the tower noticed that I was walking with a cane and directed Julieann and me to the “Fast Lane” where we walked past and least 300 people standing in line. When we arrived at the head of the line we could see why the line was moving so slowing. With all those folks waiting in line for an elevator to the top of the tower (or close to it), there were only THREE elevators to serve the masses and each elevator held, on average, SIX people. Yep, the math is staggering and once again I am thankful for my cane. I really just can't sit, crawl or stand for any length of time and without the “special” treatment offered, I would not have been able to visit the top of the tower. Two others in our group who also had some mobility problems gave up on the wait, even with the special treatment. Anyhow, Dubai is the most populous city in the United Arab Emirates and the capital of the Emirate of Dubai, the most populous of the country's seven emirates. As of 2024, the city has a population of around 3.79 million, more than 90% of which are expatriates. Beginning in the early 20th century, Dubai developed into a significant regional and international trade hub, emphasizing on tourism and luxury. Dubai has been a center for regional and international trade since the early 20th century, and its economy relies on revenues from trade, tourism, aviation, real estate, and financial services. Oil revenue helped accelerate the development of the city. However, oil production contributed less than 1 percent to the emirate's GDP in 2018. Due to financial secrecy, low taxes, and valuable real estate, Dubai is an appealing destination for money launderers, criminals, corrupt political figures and sanctioned business people to launder or hide money. The sandy desert surrounding the city supports wild grasses and occasional date palms. Several indigenous trees such as the date palm and neem as well as imported trees such as the eucalyptus grow in Dubai's natural parks. The MacQueen's bustard, striped hyena, caracal, desert fox, falcon, and Arabian oryx are common in Dubai's desert. Dubai is on the migration path between Europe, Asia, and Africa, and more than 320 migratory bird species pass through the emirate in spring and autumn. The waters of Dubai are home to more than 300 species of fish. The typical marine life off the Dubai coast includes tropical fish, jellyfish, coral, dugong, dolphins, whales, and sharks. Various types of turtles can also be found in the area including the hawks bill turtle and green turtle, which are listed as endangered species. Dubai has the second-most five-star hotels in the world and the tallest building in the world, the Burj Khalifa, which is 2,717 feet tall. Located in the eastern Arabian Peninsula, on the coast of the Persian Gulf, it is a major global transportation hub for passengers and cargo. In 2023, Dubai was the third most-visited city in the world. The "great storm" of 1908 struck the pearling boats of Dubai and the coastal emirates towards the end of the pearling season that year, resulting in the loss of a dozen boats and over 100 men. The disaster was a major setback for Dubai, with many families losing their breadwinners and merchants facing financial ruin. These losses came at a time when the tribes of the interior were also experiencing poverty. In a letter to the Sultan of Muscat in 1911, Butti laments, "Misery and poverty are raging among them, with the result that they are struggling, looting and killing among themselves." As well as undergoing growth and expansion in its regional trade links, Dubai was also an important regional center for the collection, sale and trade of pearls. The collapse of the pearling industry saw the city fall into a deep depression and many residents lived in poverty or migrated to other parts of the Persian Gulf. In 1937 an oil exploration contract was signed which guaranteed royalty rights for Dubai and concessionary payments to Sheikh Saeed bin Maktoum. However, due to World War II, oil would not be struck until 1966. In the early days since its inception, Dubai was constantly at odds with Abu Dhabi. In 1947, a border dispute between Dubai and Abu Dhabi on the northern sector of their mutual border escalated into war. Arbitration by the British government resulted in a cessation of hostilities. Despite a lack of oil, Dubai's ruler from 1958, Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum, used revenue from trading activities to build infrastructure. Private companies were established to build and operate infrastructure, including electricity, telephone services, and both the ports and airport operators. An airport of sorts (a runway built on salt flats) was established in Dubai in the 1950s and, in 1959, the emirate's first hotel, the Airlines Hotel, was constructed. This was followed by the Ambassador and Carlton Hotels in 1968. The construction of Dubai's first airport was started on the northern edge of the town in 1959 and the terminal building opened for business in September 1960. Iran Air commenced services to Shiraz in 1961. In 1962 the British Political Agent noted that "Many new houses and blocks of offices and flats are being built... the Ruler is determined, against advice [from the British authorities] to press on with the construction of a jet airport... More and more European and Arab firms are opening up and the future looks bright." In 1962, with expenditure on infrastructure projects already approaching levels some thought imprudent, Sheikh Rashid approached his brother-in-law, the Ruler of Qatar, for a loan to build the first bridge crossing Dubai Creek. This crossing was finished in May 1963 and was paid for by a toll levied on the crossing from the Dubai side of the creek to the Deira side. After years of exploration following large finds in neighboring Abu Dhabi, oil was eventually discovered in territorial waters off Dubai in 1966, albeit in far smaller quantities. Between 1968 and 1975, the city's population grew by over 300%. Dubai had already embarked on a period of infrastructural development and expansion. Oil revenue flowing from 1969 onward supported a period of growth with Sheikh Rashid embarking on a policy of building infrastructure and a diversified trading economy before the emirate's limited reserves were depleted. On 2 December 1971, Dubai, together with Abu Dhabi, Sharjah, Ajman, Umm al-Quwain, and Fujairah joined in the Act of Union to form the United Arab Emirates. The seventh emirate, Ras Al Khaimah, joined the UAE on 10 February 1972, following Iran's annexation of the RAK-claimed Tunbs islands. In 1973, Dubai joined the other emirates to adopt a uniform currency: the UAE dirham. In that same year, the prior monetary union with Qatar was dissolved and the UAE dirham was introduced throughout the Emirates. The Gulf War in early 1991 had a negative financial impact on the city, with depositors and traders withdrawing money and trade. However, Dubai rebounded in a changing political climate and prospered. Dubai served as refueling base for allied forces at the Jebel Ali Free Zone during the Gulf War and again during the 2003 Invasion of Iraq. Subsequent significant increases in oil prices prompted Dubai to maintain its focus on free trade and tourism. In the early 2000s, construction of artificial islands on the coast of Dubai, known as the Palm Islands and The World Islands, began. The Burj Khalifa was opened in Dubai in 2010, surpassing the Taipei 101 and officially gaining its title as the tallest skyscraper in the world. Dubai's smart city initiatives, which include smart tourism, play a key role in advancing the city’s growth ambitions, primarily through its Smart Dubai project. In 2016, the world's first functioning 3D-printed office building was opened in Dubai, having taken 17 days to build. Finally, I cannot leave this “information page” about Dubai without mentioning the Police Cars. Some of you, I'm sure, have heard of these Dubai cars for their Police, but for those visitors who “think” they are monetarily “well off”, “rich”, etc., this police force will make you question your own attributes. There are upwards of 40 exotic and/or unique cars and motorcycles utilized by Dubai’s police force. Here is a list of the 10 rarest, most outrageous cars in the fantastic fleet which I copied from the Internet since I know NOTHING about cars. If you're interested in seeing the photos of these cars I googled “Dubai Police Cars” for the information I now share with you. Bugatti Veyron: -- The Dubai Police are not only driving in style with the Bugatti Veyron, but they are also breaking Guinness World Records. In April 2016, they officially held the Fastest Police Car In Service record. With a top speed of 253 mph, 1,00o-horsepower, and a 16-cylinder engine that allows the car to go from 0-to-60 in only 2.5 seconds, the Bugatti Veyron is the fastest police car in Dubai and in the world. Aston Martin One-77: -- undoubtedly the rarest car of the entire Dubai police car fleet. The car costs nearly $1.8 million and is equipped with a 750-horsepower V12. It can go from 0-60 in less than 3.7 seconds and reach speeds up to 220 mph. Aston Martin only manufactured 77 of these cars, hence the name One-77. The car costs nearly $1.8 million and is equipped with a 750-horsepower V12. It can go from 0-60 in less than 3.7 seconds and reach speeds up to 220 mph. When it was revealed, it was the fastest car Aston Martin had ever built. The body of the car is made of handcrafted aluminum, giving it an incredibly sleek look. Lamborghini Aventador: The Aventador can go from 0-60 mph in 2.9 seconds with a top speed of 217 mph. At the time of purchase, it was the most expensive and fastest police car in the world. Word on the street is that the Dubai Police had this Aventador modified to go even faster than the stock ones. At the time of purchase, this beautiful car cost more than half a million dollars. Compared to the Aston Martin One-77, that’s … cheap! Porsche 918 Spyder: This gleaming vehicle can go from 0 to 60 mph in just 2.5 seconds AND is a hybrid hypercar. The 918 Spyder sports a V8 engine along with a pair of electric motors. In hybrid-electric mode, it can drive up to 22-miles. With a starting price of $895,000, but it's been mentioned that the Dubai Police “may” have added all of the gizmos and gadgets to more than likely get this car’s price tag over 1 million dollars. There were fewer than 1,000 examples of this car made, making this car one of the rarest and most expensive of the fleet. Ferrari FF: The front of this car fully looks like a Ferrari. But, the back half looks more like a station wagon and it has four seats! The FF has a top speed of 208 mph and can go from 0-to-60 mph in just 3.7 seconds. The price at the time of purchase in 2013 was a little over half a million. BMW i8: It’s a hybrid sports car. Though a BMW may not feel as impressive as some of the other super cars on the list, the i8 can certainly keep up with its fellow comrades in the fleet. With a starting price of $135,925, this car is on the “cheaper” end for the Dubai Police. But, this car was perfect for their goal of being more environmentally conscious. The i8 can go from 0-to-60 mph in 4.4 seconds. It has a top speed of 155 mph. Along with that, with a fully charged battery back, the i8 has an all-electric, zero-emission range of 22-miles. Mercedes AMG GT 63S: In December of 2019, the Dubai Police added the Mercedes AMG GT 63S, priced at nearly $200,000. This vehicle took their “top spot of the four-door model range” according to the Dubai Police HQ Instagram. It can reach 62 mph in 3.2 seconds and tops out at 195 mph. It’s equipped with a four-liter V8 engine. Brabus B63S-700 6×6 Widestar: Some call it the Brabus B63S-700 6×6 Widestar, others call it a G-Wagon on steroids. In 2013, the Dubai Police brought on this monstrosity of a super-truck, equipped with 700 horsepower and costing $780,000+. The body of the vehicle is based on the Mercedes-Benz G63 AMG. It’s able to go from 0-to-60 mph in 7.4 seconds and has a top speed of 100 mph. McLaren MP4 12C: Coming in at more than $200,000, the McLaren MP4 12C supercar is equipped with a 3.8-liter twin-turbo V8 engine. The British supercar comes with 616 horsepower and 445 ft.-lb. torque. This speed racer goes from 0-to-60 mph in just 3.1 seconds. And the icing on the cake? It can do 200 mph. Tesla Cybertruck: Credit: Dubai Police HQ via Twitter The Dubai Police tweeted about adding the Tesla Cybertruck to their fleet as soon as it becomes available. Although the expected release date isn’t decided, this Tesla is just too futuristic and on-brand for Dubai not to add. The triple-motor range-topper will cost $69,000. And it is safe to say that the Dubai Police will be purchasing that high-end version. With that price tag, the Dubai police will probably be ordering a LOT of Tesla Cybertrucks. And let’s just say, I’m sure Elon Musk is happy about it. Dubai Police Fleet Honorable Mentions: Dubai Police Hoverbikes: Hoverbikes? If you are thinking that perhaps that word means a motorcycle that flies, you aren’t crazy. That’s right, the Dubai Police Force is currently testing out flying motorcycles. It seems, however, that they are nowhere near ready to use them. Recently, a crash during a test drive was caught on video. Luckily, the test driver was not hurt. In the video, you can see that the bike is surrounded by spinning propellers — it looks like a death trap. And for that, I’d say, let’s just stick to motorcycles that stay on the road. Isn’t that dangerous enough? They have had some successful attempts at flying this contraption … just closer to the ground. Hoversurf is a California-based company. Their hoverbike concept has a 12.3 kWh hybrid battery, allowing for a flying time in drone mode of up to 40 minutes. While the initial thought of a flying motorcycle sounds futuristic and incredible, the dangers of the Hoversurf seem endless. According to Dan Mihalascu, “The S3 2019 needs a recharge time of 2.5 hours for every 25 minutes of use, and the maximum flight speed is just 60 mph (96 km/h). Need more? The four carbon propellers are dangerously close to the rider but the Dubai Police says there will be safety rings around them for protection.” So, maybe we aren’t rooting for flying motorcycles after all. Still really cool though, right? Electric Dubai Police Cars: In 2017, the Dubai Police Department added the BMW i3 electric car to its fleet. While it doesn’t have that classic wow factor that the other super cars in the fleet have, it does have the great-for-your-environment factor that is needed to stay relevant and respectable, especially in a city like Dubai. The BMW i3 has a fully emission-free electric motor specifically made for city driving. The car itself is also made of renewable and recyclable material. It has a maximum 181 horsepower. The United Arab Emirates energy minister, Suhail Al-Mazrouei, hopes to have electric cars make up 10% of all government vehicles in the next four years to reduce CO2 emissions. If you’re looking for an argument on if being environmentally conscious is in style, this is your sign. If the Dubai Police are taking part, there’s no question on the coolness. W Motors Ghiath: All of the cars we’ve highlighted so far have been unarguably beautiful and sleek. Well, the W Motors Ghiath is a whole different monster. The Ghiath is neither sleek or beautiful — in fact this car just looks scary. Forget about it’s intimidating styling, because this vehicle is said to be the most advanced police car in the world. It’s equipped with onboard AI and facial recognition systems. There is a communication system that links directly to a Command Operations Room. Dubai Drone Taxi: Though the Dubai Police don’t plan to add drone taxis to their force, we couldn’t talk about all the insanity surrounding cars and technology in Dubai without mentioning the Dubai Drone Taxis. And no, we aren’t in a simulation. This is real. In a BBC article, Jane Wakefield reported, “The drone was designed by German firm Volocopter and the firm said it hopes to have the taxis up and running within five years.” Well, that was reported in 2017. Flying in the sky without a person driving? If it sounds outrageous, you’ll probably hear about it happening first in Dubai.

As well as undergoing growth and expansion in its regional trade links, Dubai was also an important regional center for the collection, sale and trade of pearls. The collapse of the pearling industry saw the city fall into a deep depression and many residents lived in poverty or migrated to other parts of the Persian Gulf. In 1937 an oil exploration contract was signed which guaranteed royalty rights for Dubai and concessionary payments to Sheikh Saeed bin Maktoum. However, due to World War II, oil would not be struck until 1966. In the early days since its inception, Dubai was constantly at odds with Abu Dhabi. In 1947, a border dispute between Dubai and Abu Dhabi on the northern sector of their mutual border escalated into war. Arbitration by the British government resulted in a cessation of hostilities. Despite a lack of oil, Dubai's ruler from 1958, Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum, used revenue from trading activities to build infrastructure. Private companies were established to build and operate infrastructure, including electricity, telephone services, and both the ports and airport operators. An airport of sorts (a runway built on salt flats) was established in Dubai in the 1950s and, in 1959, the emirate's first hotel, the Airlines Hotel, was constructed. This was followed by the Ambassador and Carlton Hotels in 1968. The construction of Dubai's first airport was started on the northern edge of the town in 1959 and the terminal building opened for business in September 1960. Iran Air commenced services to Shiraz in 1961. In 1962 the British Political Agent noted that "Many new houses and blocks of offices and flats are being built... the Ruler is determined, against advice [from the British authorities] to press on with the construction of a jet airport... More and more European and Arab firms are opening up and the future looks bright." In 1962, with expenditure on infrastructure projects already approaching levels some thought imprudent, Sheikh Rashid approached his brother-in-law, the Ruler of Qatar, for a loan to build the first bridge crossing Dubai Creek. This crossing was finished in May 1963 and was paid for by a toll levied on the crossing from the Dubai side of the creek to the Deira side. After years of exploration following large finds in neighboring Abu Dhabi, oil was eventually discovered in territorial waters off Dubai in 1966, albeit in far smaller quantities. Between 1968 and 1975, the city's population grew by over 300%. Dubai had already embarked on a period of infrastructural development and expansion. Oil revenue flowing from 1969 onward supported a period of growth with Sheikh Rashid embarking on a policy of building infrastructure and a diversified trading economy before the emirate's limited reserves were depleted. On 2 December 1971, Dubai, together with Abu Dhabi, Sharjah, Ajman, Umm al-Quwain, and Fujairah joined in the Act of Union to form the United Arab Emirates. The seventh emirate, Ras Al Khaimah, joined the UAE on 10 February 1972, following Iran's annexation of the RAK-claimed Tunbs islands. In 1973, Dubai joined the other emirates to adopt a uniform currency: the UAE dirham. In that same year, the prior monetary union with Qatar was dissolved and the UAE dirham was introduced throughout the Emirates. The Gulf War in early 1991 had a negative financial impact on the city, with depositors and traders withdrawing money and trade. However, Dubai rebounded in a changing political climate and prospered. Dubai served as refueling base for allied forces at the Jebel Ali Free Zone during the Gulf War and again during the 2003 Invasion of Iraq. Subsequent significant increases in oil prices prompted Dubai to maintain its focus on free trade and tourism. In the early 2000s, construction of artificial islands on the coast of Dubai, known as the Palm Islands and The World Islands, began. The Burj Khalifa was opened in Dubai in 2010, surpassing the Taipei 101 and officially gaining its title as the tallest skyscraper in the world. Dubai's smart city initiatives, which include smart tourism, play a key role in advancing the city’s growth ambitions, primarily through its Smart Dubai project. In 2016, the world's first functioning 3D-printed office building was opened in Dubai, having taken 17 days to build. Finally, I cannot leave this “information page” about Dubai without mentioning the Police Cars. Some of you, I'm sure, have heard of these Dubai cars for their Police, but for those visitors who “think” they are monetarily “well off”, “rich”, etc., this police force will make you question your own attributes. There are upwards of 40 exotic and/or unique cars and motorcycles utilized by Dubai’s police force. Here is a list of the 10 rarest, most outrageous cars in the fantastic fleet which I copied from the Internet since I know NOTHING about cars. If you're interested in seeing the photos of these cars I googled “Dubai Police Cars” for the information I now share with you. Bugatti Veyron: -- The Dubai Police are not only driving in style with the Bugatti Veyron, but they are also breaking Guinness World Records. In April 2016, they officially held the Fastest Police Car In Service record. With a top speed of 253 mph, 1,00o-horsepower, and a 16-cylinder engine that allows the car to go from 0-to-60 in only 2.5 seconds, the Bugatti Veyron is the fastest police car in Dubai and in the world. Aston Martin One-77: -- undoubtedly the rarest car of the entire Dubai police car fleet. The car costs nearly $1.8 million and is equipped with a 750-horsepower V12. It can go from 0-60 in less than 3.7 seconds and reach speeds up to 220 mph. Aston Martin only manufactured 77 of these cars, hence the name One-77. The car costs nearly $1.8 million and is equipped with a 750-horsepower V12. It can go from 0-60 in less than 3.7 seconds and reach speeds up to 220 mph. When it was revealed, it was the fastest car Aston Martin had ever built. The body of the car is made of handcrafted aluminum, giving it an incredibly sleek look. Lamborghini Aventador: The Aventador can go from 0-60 mph in 2.9 seconds with a top speed of 217 mph. At the time of purchase, it was the most expensive and fastest police car in the world. Word on the street is that the Dubai Police had this Aventador modified to go even faster than the stock ones. At the time of purchase, this beautiful car cost more than half a million dollars. Compared to the Aston Martin One-77, that’s … cheap! Porsche 918 Spyder: This gleaming vehicle can go from 0 to 60 mph in just 2.5 seconds AND is a hybrid hypercar. The 918 Spyder sports a V8 engine along with a pair of electric motors. In hybrid-electric mode, it can drive up to 22-miles. With a starting price of $895,000, but it's been mentioned that the Dubai Police “may” have added all of the gizmos and gadgets to more than likely get this car’s price tag over 1 million dollars. There were fewer than 1,000 examples of this car made, making this car one of the rarest and most expensive of the fleet. Ferrari FF: The front of this car fully looks like a Ferrari. But, the back half looks more like a station wagon and it has four seats! The FF has a top speed of 208 mph and can go from 0-to-60 mph in just 3.7 seconds. The price at the time of purchase in 2013 was a little over half a million. BMW i8: It’s a hybrid sports car. Though a BMW may not feel as impressive as some of the other super cars on the list, the i8 can certainly keep up with its fellow comrades in the fleet. With a starting price of $135,925, this car is on the “cheaper” end for the Dubai Police. But, this car was perfect for their goal of being more environmentally conscious. The i8 can go from 0-to-60 mph in 4.4 seconds. It has a top speed of 155 mph. Along with that, with a fully charged battery back, the i8 has an all-electric, zero-emission range of 22-miles. Mercedes AMG GT 63S: In December of 2019, the Dubai Police added the Mercedes AMG GT 63S, priced at nearly $200,000. This vehicle took their “top spot of the four-door model range” according to the Dubai Police HQ Instagram. It can reach 62 mph in 3.2 seconds and tops out at 195 mph. It’s equipped with a four-liter V8 engine. Brabus B63S-700 6×6 Widestar: Some call it the Brabus B63S-700 6×6 Widestar, others call it a G-Wagon on steroids. In 2013, the Dubai Police brought on this monstrosity of a super-truck, equipped with 700 horsepower and costing $780,000+. The body of the vehicle is based on the Mercedes-Benz G63 AMG. It’s able to go from 0-to-60 mph in 7.4 seconds and has a top speed of 100 mph. McLaren MP4 12C: Coming in at more than $200,000, the McLaren MP4 12C supercar is equipped with a 3.8-liter twin-turbo V8 engine. The British supercar comes with 616 horsepower and 445 ft.-lb. torque. This speed racer goes from 0-to-60 mph in just 3.1 seconds. And the icing on the cake? It can do 200 mph. Tesla Cybertruck: Credit: Dubai Police HQ via Twitter The Dubai Police tweeted about adding the Tesla Cybertruck to their fleet as soon as it becomes available. Although the expected release date isn’t decided, this Tesla is just too futuristic and on-brand for Dubai not to add. The triple-motor range-topper will cost $69,000. And it is safe to say that the Dubai Police will be purchasing that high-end version. With that price tag, the Dubai police will probably be ordering a LOT of Tesla Cybertrucks. And let’s just say, I’m sure Elon Musk is happy about it. Dubai Police Fleet Honorable Mentions: Dubai Police Hoverbikes: Hoverbikes? If you are thinking that perhaps that word means a motorcycle that flies, you aren’t crazy. That’s right, the Dubai Police Force is currently testing out flying motorcycles. It seems, however, that they are nowhere near ready to use them. Recently, a crash during a test drive was caught on video. Luckily, the test driver was not hurt. In the video, you can see that the bike is surrounded by spinning propellers — it looks like a death trap. And for that, I’d say, let’s just stick to motorcycles that stay on the road. Isn’t that dangerous enough? They have had some successful attempts at flying this contraption … just closer to the ground. Hoversurf is a California-based company. Their hoverbike concept has a 12.3 kWh hybrid battery, allowing for a flying time in drone mode of up to 40 minutes. While the initial thought of a flying motorcycle sounds futuristic and incredible, the dangers of the Hoversurf seem endless. According to Dan Mihalascu, “The S3 2019 needs a recharge time of 2.5 hours for every 25 minutes of use, and the maximum flight speed is just 60 mph (96 km/h). Need more? The four carbon propellers are dangerously close to the rider but the Dubai Police says there will be safety rings around them for protection.” So, maybe we aren’t rooting for flying motorcycles after all. Still really cool though, right? Electric Dubai Police Cars: In 2017, the Dubai Police Department added the BMW i3 electric car to its fleet. While it doesn’t have that classic wow factor that the other super cars in the fleet have, it does have the great-for-your-environment factor that is needed to stay relevant and respectable, especially in a city like Dubai. The BMW i3 has a fully emission-free electric motor specifically made for city driving. The car itself is also made of renewable and recyclable material. It has a maximum 181 horsepower. The United Arab Emirates energy minister, Suhail Al-Mazrouei, hopes to have electric cars make up 10% of all government vehicles in the next four years to reduce CO2 emissions. If you’re looking for an argument on if being environmentally conscious is in style, this is your sign. If the Dubai Police are taking part, there’s no question on the coolness. W Motors Ghiath: All of the cars we’ve highlighted so far have been unarguably beautiful and sleek. Well, the W Motors Ghiath is a whole different monster. The Ghiath is neither sleek or beautiful — in fact this car just looks scary. Forget about it’s intimidating styling, because this vehicle is said to be the most advanced police car in the world. It’s equipped with onboard AI and facial recognition systems. There is a communication system that links directly to a Command Operations Room. Dubai Drone Taxi: Though the Dubai Police don’t plan to add drone taxis to their force, we couldn’t talk about all the insanity surrounding cars and technology in Dubai without mentioning the Dubai Drone Taxis. And no, we aren’t in a simulation. This is real. In a BBC article, Jane Wakefield reported, “The drone was designed by German firm Volocopter and the firm said it hopes to have the taxis up and running within five years.” Well, that was reported in 2017. Flying in the sky without a person driving? If it sounds outrageous, you’ll probably hear about it happening first in Dubai.

key_sentiment_drivers: Transformation From A Small Fishing Village To A Global City
posts.p_message: 𝔻𝕌𝔹𝔸𝕀 ඩුබායි ඉතිහාසය කුඩා ධීවර ගම්මානයක සිට ලෝකයේ අතිශය දියුණු නගරයක් වූ ( megapolis ) ඩුබායි රාජ්‍යයේ ඉතිහාස කථාව --------------------------- අටසීයක පිරිසකගෙන් නිර්මාණය වූ ධීවර සහ මුතු කිමිදිම / වෙළඳාම් ජීවනෝපාය කරගත් කුඩා සරල ගම්මානයක් පසු කාලයේ සඳ මතට සහ අඟහරු වෙත රොකට් යවමින් සහ වෙනත් විශ්මිත නිර්මාණ එළිදක්වමින් ලෝකයෙහි ම ආකර්ෂණය දිනාගැනීම සිතා ගැනීමට පවා නොහැකිය. එහෙත් එහි පාලකයින් එය කළ නොහැකි කාර්යයක්‌ නොවන බව මනාව ඔප්පු කොට ඇත. එසේම සම්පත්, අදායම් සහ ප්‍රතිලාභ පාලකයන් පිරිසට සහ ඔවුන් වටා සිටින පිරිසක් පමණක් නොව රටවැසියාට ද භුක්ති විඳිමට අවස්ථාව සලසා දීම සහ ඇතැම් රටවල දේශපාලනඥයන් ගිරිය කඩාගෙන " මතු උපදින පරපුරට අපි යමක් කළ යුතු යයි '' පාරම්නොබා එය ක්‍රියාවෙන් ඔප්පු කර ඇත.වසර 12 කට වැඩි කාලයක් මෙහි ජිවත් වන මා රැකියාව කරන ස්ථානය අනුව මෙම රටේ ඉහළම පිරිසේ සිට සෑම තරාතිරමේ පුද්ගලයින් දැක හඳුනා ගෙන ඇත. කිසිවකු රැකියාවට මෙහි පැමිණ වැරදිලා හො අමිහිරි අත්දැකීම් මෙන්ම වැටුප් පිලිබඳ ප්‍රශ්න වලට මුහුණ දෙනවා නම් මගෙ අත්දැකීම් නම් ඔහු වැඩ කරනවා ඇත්තේ දකුණු ආසියාතික රටක නිලධාරීන්ගේ බලය වැඩි ආයතනයක විය යුතුය.ඔවුන් අතරද ද යහපත් මිනිසුන් සිටිති.ස්වදේශීය මිනිස්සු ගෙ ගුණාංග බොහො ලිවිය හැකි මුත් මෙතැන් සිට කථාව ලියන්නෙමි. ""ඩුබායි '' නම හැදුනු හැටි පිලිබඳ විව්ධ මතයන් ඇති අතර එයින් වඩාත් ප්‍රචලිත souq හෙවත් විවෘත වෙළඳ මාධ්‍යස්ථානය හෙවත් නොයෙකුත් කුඩා කඩ සාප්පු පිරුණු ස්ථානය යන්න ය. තවත් විදියකින් අරාබි පිරුලක් වන " ඩාබා ඩුබායි " ලෙස දැක්විය හැකිය.තේරුම " ඔවුන් ඇවිල්ලා තියෙන්නේ මුදල් ගොඩක් සමගය '' යන්න ය. ඩුබායි ගැන ඉතිහාසය සහ සංස්කෘතිය ගැන අධ්‍යයනය කළ Fedel Handhal ශාස්ත්‍රඥයා ට අනුව "ඩුබායි' පැමිණ ඇත්තේ අරාබි වචනයක් වන yadub කියන වචනයෙනි. එහි අතීත පදය dabba ලෙස විය හැකිය. සෙමින් ගලා යාම එහි තේරුම ය. එනම් ඩුබායි ඇළ නිසා එය යෙදෙන්නට ඇත.කවියෙක් සහ ශාස්ත්‍රඥයෙක් වන අහාමඩ් මොහොමඩ් ඔබයිඩ් මෙම වචනය ම සොයාගත්තත් ඔහුගේ අදහස මිනිස් ජනාවාස ඇරඹ්මට ප්‍රථම මෙම ප්‍රදේශයේ බහුලව කුඩා පළගැටියන් ( baby locust ) සිටි නිසා මෙම නම යෙදුණු බවයි. ඩුබායි යනු එක්සත් අරාබි එමීර් රාජ්‍යයේ ඇති එමිරේට් හතෙන් එකකි ඩුබායි රාජ්‍යය අරාබි අර්ධද්වීපයේ පර්සියානු බොක්කෙහි දකුණට වන්නට පිහිටා ඇත.භූමි ප්‍රමාණය අනුව ( වර්ග සැතපුම් 1588.4)අබුඩාබි වලට දෙවැනි වුවත් විශාලතම ජනගහනය ජිවත් වන්නේ මෙහි ය. (අබුඩාබිය ඩුබායි මෙන් 17 ගුණයක් විශාල ය 84%) අරාබි එමීර් රාජ්‍යයට අයත් අනෙක් රාජ්‍යයන් නම් සාජා, අජ්මාන්, උම් -අල් කයිම්, රස් -අල් කයිමා සහ ෆුජයිරා වෙයි. වර්ෂ 1971 ට පෙර ඩුබායි ඇතුළු මේ සියලු පළාත් හැඳින් වුයේ "සටන් විරාමයක් ලද රාජ්‍යයක් '' ලෙසය (TRUCIAL STATES ) 19 වන ශත වර්ෂයේ දි මෙම සටන් විරාමය එක්සත් රාජධානිය සහ අරාබි ශීක් ජාතිකයින් අතර පැවතුනි. මේ වන විට ලෝකයේ 7 වන විශාලතම තෙල් සංරක්ෂිතය හා මැද පෙරදිග වඩාත් සංවර්ධිත ආර්ථිකය එරටට හිමිව තිබේ. පෙඩරල් ජාතික කවුන්සලය තුළ රටේ තීරණාත්මක කරුණු කාරණා වලදී නිශේධ බලය ඇත්තේ ඩුබායි සහ අබුඩාබි රාජ්‍යයන් දෙකටය. එසේ වැඩි බලයක් සහ මෙතරම් දියුණුවක් ලබා ගත් ආකාරය ගැන වැඩි විස්තර මෙහි ඇතුලත් කරන්නෙමි. ඩුබායි පිලිබඳ මුල්ම ලිඛිත සඳහන ක්‍රි ව 1095 දි හමුවන අතර ඩුබායි නගරය නමැති මුල්ම ජනාවාසය පිලිබඳ 1799 සිට දැනුවත්ව ඇත.1822 දි පමණ බානියස් (Bani yas වරිගය )පෙළපතේ 700-800පමණ පිරිසක් මෙහි ධීවර කටයුතු වල යෙදෙමින් පදිංචිව සිට ඇත. ඔවුන්ගේ රටේ පාලකයා ලෙස අබුඩාබි ෂෙයික් තහනුන් බින් ශක්බුට් ( sheikh Tehnun bin shakhbut ) සලකා තිබෙ. වර්ෂ 1822 බ්‍රිතාන්‍ය නාවික නිරීක්ෂකයෙකුට අනුව එවකට මෙම ප්‍රාදේශය ඉලිප්සාකාර හැඩයට තිබෙන බිම් තීරුවක නගරය මිනිස්සු දහසක් පමණ ජීවත්ව සිට ඇත.එය මැටි තාප්පයකින් වට වී තිබුණි.එහි එළුවන් සහ ඔටුවන් සිටි අතර ප්‍රධාන අඩි පාර ආසන්නයේ ඇළ මාර්ගය දෙසටත් කාන්තාරය දෙසටත් යන තවලම් මාර්ග 2ක්‌ දක්නට තිබුණි. 1833 දි ඔවුන්ගේ වර්ගයේ ආරවුලක් නිසා අල් බු ෆලාසි ( Al abu Falasa )සාමාජිකයින් පිරිසක් අබුඩාබි සිට පැමිණ මෙහි ස්ථිර ජනාවාස පිහිටුවා ගත් අතර මෙම පිරිසේ මුලිකත්වය ගත්තේ ඔබෙයිඩ් බින් සායිඩ් ( Obeid bin saeed ) සහ මක්තුම් බින් බටි ( Maktoum bin butti )යන දෙදෙනා ය.ඔවුන් දෙදෙනා නව නගරයේ (ඩුබායි )සම නායකත්වය ( joint leaders )ඉසූලු අතර ඔබෙයිඩ් බින් සයිඩ් ක්‍රි ව 1836 දි වයස නිසා මිය යාමත් සමග මක්තුම් රාජවංශය ප්‍රතිෂ්ඨාපිත විය. එය අඛණ්ඩව අද දක්වා පවතී.ක්‍රි ව 1892 දි එක්සත් රාජධානිය ඩුබායි හි ආරක්‍ෂාව භාරගන්නා ලදී. මෙම ප්‍රදේශයේ ඇති භූගෝල පිහිටීම අනුව එය වැදගත් වෙළඳ මධ්‍යස්ථානයක් බවට පත් කෙරුණු අතර 20 වන සියවසේ ආරම්භය වන විට එය වැදගත් වරායක් බවට පත්ව තිබුණි. මෙතෙක් භාවිතා කළ (ඉන්දියානු රජය නිකුත් කළ )පර්සියන් ගල්ෆ් රුපියල වෙනුවට අළුතින් නිදහස (1966 දි ) ලැබූ කටාර් රාජ්‍යය සහ තවමත් නගරයක් වන ඩුබායි නව මුදල් ඒකකයක් ( රියල් )භාවිතයට ගත් අතර (අබුඩාබි පළාත කුවේට් ඩිනාර් භාවිතා කරන ලදී. මෙම වර්ෂයේදී ඩුබායි තෙල් සොයා ගන්නා ලද අතර මෙම තෙල් ආර්ථිකය නිසා විශාල විදේශීය සේවක ආගමනයක් සිදුවිය. නගරය 300% විහිදුමට ලක්‌ වී තෙල් සම්බන්ධ නවීන තාක්ෂනය සහ පිරිස පැමිණෙන්නට විය. මේ අළුත් ඩුබායි එමිරේටය නිර්මාණය කරන ලද්දේ 1971 එක්සත් රාජධානිය ප්‍රදේශය හැර දා යෑමෙන් පසුවය. ඩුබායි, අබුඩාබි සහ අනෙකුත් රාජ්‍යයන් පහ එකතුව සෑදු අරාබි එමීර් රාජ්‍යය 1979 දි ජබල් අලි නිදහස් වෙළඳ කලාපය පිහිටුවා විදේශ සමාගම් වලට සිමාවන් තහංචි රහිතව ශ්‍රාමිකයන් ආනයනය කිරීමට සහ සම්පත් අපනයනය සඳහා ඉඩ ලබා දෙන ලදී පසු කාලයේ තෙල් ප්‍රධානතම ආර්ථික සාධකය වන්නට නොදී සංචාරක කර්මාන්තය ස්වභාව සෞන්දර්ය සහ වෙළඳ ව්‍යාපාරයන් මුල් තැනට පත් කරන ලදී ඩුබායි පාලකයින් -:( මුල සිට ) 01- ෂෙයික් මක්තුම් බින් බටි සොහෙල් ( Sheikh Maktoum bin butti bin sohail ) අබුඩාබි සිට Obeid bin saeed bin Rashid සමග සංක්‍රමණය වී සම නායකත්වය ගෙන ඩුබායි නිර්මාණය කරන ලදී.ඔහුගේ පියා බටි බින් සොහෙල් ය.පාලන කාලය 1833-1852. මුහුදු ගමනක දී මස්කට් සිට Qishm ( ඉරාන නගරයක් ) වසුරිය රෝගය නිසා 1852 දි මිය යන ලදි. 02- ෂෙයික් සයිඩ් බින් බටි ( sheikh Saeed bin butti) ඩුබායි තුන් වන පාලකයා වන අතර ෂෙයික් බටි බින් සොහාල් ගෙ පුත්‍රයා ය. ඔහුද 1859 දි වසුරිය නිසා මිය ගොස් ඇත. 03- ෂෙයික් හෂෙර් බින් මක්තුම් බින් බටි අල් මක්තු ම් ( sheikh Hasher bin Maktoum bin butti al Maktoum )1859 සිට 1886 මොහු කලින් අනපේක්ෂිතව මිය ගිය ෂෙයික් සයිඩ් බටි ගෙ ඥාති පුත්‍රයා ය 04- ෂෙයික් රෂිඩ් බින් මක්තුම් ( sheikh Rashid bin Maktoum ) රාජ්‍ය පාලනය 1886 සිට 1894. කලින් පාලක ෂෙයික් හෂෙර් මිය ගියේ ඉදිරි පාලකයා නම් නොකරය. මෙම නිසා පවුලේ සාමාජිකයින් ගෙන් දෙදෙනෙක් රජකමට තරඟ කිරීමට සිටි. ඒ ෂෙයික් හෂෙල් ගෙ වැඩිමල් පුත් ෂෙයික් මක්තුම් බින් හෂෙර් සහ ෂෙයික් රෂිඩ් බින් මක්තුම් වන ෂෙයික් හෂෙල් ගෙ සහෝදරයා ය මෙහිදී රජ පවුලේ බහුතරයක් ගෙ තේරීම ෂෙයික් රෂිඩ් ට ලැබුණි. ( එහෙත් සිදුවීම් බොහොමයකට මුහුණ පාන්නට සිදු විය **) 1894 අප්‍රේල් 07 ඩුබායි මෙකල කොලරා වසංගතේ පැතිරිලා තිබුනත් මරණයට හේතුව අංශභාගය යයි සඳහන් වෙයි. 05- ෂෙයික් මක්තුම් බින් හෂෙර් අල් මක්තුම් ( Sheikh Maktoum bin Hasher AL Maktoum )1894 අප්‍රේල් සිට 1906 පෙබරවාරි දක්වා. මක්තුම් වංශයේ පස්වනියා ය lingeh (ඉරාන් ) පළාතේ බදු පැනවීම නිසා අධෛර්යට පත් වෙළෙඳන් ඩුබායි දෙසට යොමුකර ගන්නට සමත් වූ නිදහස් මධ්‍යස්ථ මතධාරියෙක් ලෙස සැලකේ.එසේම මෙමගින් ඔහුගේ පාලන කාලය තුළ ඩුබායි වෙරළබඩ වරාය වැදගත් වෙළඳ මධ්‍යස්ථානයක් බවට පත් විය.එසේම මුතු වෙලඳම නිසා ස්ථිර රැකියාවන් 7000 ඇතිකරවන ලදී 1906 පෙබරවාරි 16 ඔහු මිය යන්නේ හෘද රෝගයෙනි.( නැවක් ගිල්ලවීම සහ වන්දි ගෙවීමේ සිද්ධිය මත ඩුබායි ප්‍රතිලාබ හිමි වීමද සිදු වුයේ මේකි කාලයේ බව සඳහන් වෙයි. 06- ෂෙයික් බටි බින් සුහල් අල් මක්තුම් ( sheikh Butti bin Sohail Al Maktoum ) 1906 පෙබරවාරි 16 - සිට 1912 නොවැම්බර් 12 හෘද රෝගයෙන් මියගිය සිය ඥාති සහෝදර මක්ටුම් බින් හෂෙර් ගෙ පසු පාලන බලය හිමි කරගත් මොහු නිදහස් ප්‍රතිපත්ති ක්‍රියාත්මක කරමින් ඩුබායි වෙළඳ වරාය පුළුල් කරන ලදී. (මරණය ස්වභාවික ය ) 07- සහිඩ් බින් මක්තුම් බින් හෂෙර් අල් මක්තුම් ( Saeed bin Maktoum bin Hasher Al Maktoum )1912 සිට 1958 මිය යන තුරු රාජ්‍ය පාලනය හෙබ වූ ඔහු මුතු වෙළඳාම කඩා වැටීම හේතු කොට ගෙන ඇතිවූ ආර්ථික අර්බුදය නිම කිරීමට වෙනත් විකල්ප සඳහා යොමු විය. 08- ෂෙයික් රෂිඩ් බින් සායිඩ් අල් මක්තුම් ( sheikh Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum ) 1971 දෙසැම්බර් 02 සිට 1990 ඔක්තෝම්බර් 07 දක්වා.එක්සත් අරාබි එමිරේට්ස් නිර්මාතෘවරුන් ගෙන් කෙනෙකි. අවුරුදු 32 ඩුබායි පාලකයා වූ අතර UAE පළමු උප ජනාධිපති වරයා මෙන්ම එහි දෙවන අගමැති වරයා ද විය (30/04/1979 සිට ) ඔහුගෙන් පසු පත්වන සෑම අගමැති වරයෙක් ම සම්ප්‍රදායානුකුලව EMIRATES හි උප ජනාධිපති වරයා බවට පත් වෙයි. මොහුගේ පාලන කාලය තුළ ඩුබායි ඇළ මාර්ගය සංවර්ධනය කොට නුතන වරාය නගරය ප්‍රධාන වෙළඳ කේන්දස්ථානයක්‌ බවට පත් කරන ලදී.වර්ෂ 1966 දි ඩුබායි තෙල් නිධී සොයා ගත් අතර 1969 එහි නිෂ්පාදන සහ බෙදා හැරීම ආරම්භ කරන ලදී. විචක්ෂණ බුද්ධියක් තිබු ඔහු පරම්පරා කිහිපයකින් මෙම සම්පත නිම වන බව වටහා ගත්තේය. මේ ඔහුගේ ප්‍රචලිත කියමනකි. "" මගේ සීයා ඔටුවා පැද්දා. මගේ තාත්තත් ඔටුවා මත ගමන් කළාය. මම මර්සිඩිස් එලවනවා, මගේ පුතා ලෑන්ඩ් රොවර් පදවාවී ය, ඔහුගේ පුතත් ලෑන්ඩ් රොවර් පදවාවී එහෙත් එයාගේ පුතා ආයෙත් ඔටුවා පිටේ යාවී ' එසේ කි මේ පාලකයා අනාගතේ සඳහා සුදානම් විය. මේ ඔටුවා ගෙ කථාව පිලිබඳ සමහර අය විසින් කරන ලද අර්ථ කථනය ද මා දැක ඇත. එනම් නවීන තාක්ෂනයේ දියුණුව නිසා හති වැටෙන මිනිසා නැවතත් සරල ජීවිතයට පිවිසෙනවා යන්න ය . 09- ෂෙයික් මක්තුම් බින් රෂිඩ් ( sheikh Maktoum bin Rashid Al Maktoum ) 1990-2006 ෂෙයික් රෂිඩ් බින් සායිඩ් අල් මක්තුම් ඩුබායි පාලකයාගේ පුත්‍රයා ය. Uae හි දෙවැනි උප ජනාධිපති මෙන්ම පළමු සහ තුන් වන අගමැති වරයාද විය. 10- ෂෙයික් මොහමඩ් බින් රෂිඩ් අල් මක්තුම් ( sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum )වර්ෂ 2006 සිට මේ දක්වා. ෂෙයික් රෂිඩ් බින් සායිඩ් අල් මක්තුම් ගෙ තවත් පුත්‍රයෙකි. එසේම සිය මුතුන් මිත්තන් 1833 සිට ධීවර රැකිය මුතු කිමිදිම් ආදියෙන් පටන් ගත් සරල ගම්මානයේ සිට මේ වන විට සඳ සහ අඟහරු මතට රොකට් යැවීම දක්වා රට තුළ දියුණුව වේගවත් කළා යැයි සඳහන් කිරීම නිවැරදිය.1980 මැද භාගයේ ගල්ෆ් එයාර් ගුවන් සේවය සේවා සැපයීම නැවැත්වූ විට ඉන් අධෛර්යට පත් නොවී පකිස්තාන් ගුවන් සමාගමෙන් සැපයු ගුවන් යනා දෙක සහ පුහුණු පහසුකම් සමග 1985 මාර්තු 15 ඇරඹි එමිරේටස් ගුවන් සමාගම මේ වන විට ලක්ෂයකට අධික සේවකයින් සිටින ලෝකයේ හතර වන විශාලතම ගුවන් සමාගම බවට පත් වී ඇත. ලෝකයේ රටවල් 80 ක නගර 150 කට සතියක දි ගුවන් ගමන් 3600 වඩා සිදු කරනු ලැබේ. එදා ගුවන් යානා දෙකක් වුවත් අද එය
